content:
I grew up believing I was important. Not in the way that all children believe they are important, which is a phase that corrects itself when the world provides sufficient evidence to the contrary. I believed I was important in a specific, structural, inherited way. My father was court wizard. My grandfather was court wizard. My great-grandfather was court wizard. The line stretched back through forty thousand years of service, each man a descendant of Blake, each man carrying the same bloodline gift that made our magic stronger than any mortal wizard's, each man standing beside the throne of Godric as protector, advisor, and arcane shield. That was the word my father used. Shield. "We are the King's shield, Gorm. His magical defense. He is strong, yes, the strongest there is, but strength is not magic, and magic is our domain, and our domain is where he needs us."

I believed him. I was twelve years old when he said it, sitting cross-legged on the floor of his study while he catalogued enchanted artifacts, and the sentence lodged in my brain like a seed and grew roots that reached into every part of my identity. I was going to be a shield. I was going to protect Uncle Godric. Uncle Godric, who had been at every one of my birthday celebrations since I could remember, who ruffled my hair and asked about my studies and laughed at my jokes with a warmth that made me feel like the most important person in the room. Uncle Godric, who sat in his chair and read reports and governed the omniverse with the quiet competence of a man who has been doing his job for a very long time and is good at it. Uncle Godric, who was strong, yes, everyone knew that, but who kept court wizards because court wizards provided the magical expertise that a strong king, even the strongest king, could not provide alone.

The logic was sound. It made sense. It was also, I would learn at age twenty, the most wrong thing anyone had ever believed about anything in the entire history of the omniverse.

I turned twenty three months before the incident. The Awakening took hold on my birthday, the same way it took hold for every Thronegate man, and the difference between my gift and the standard gift was something I noticed immediately but did not understand. Standard Awakening multiplied a man's strength, endurance, intelligence, and wisdom tenfold daily. My gift multiplied them a thousandfold daily. I did not know why. My father did not explain. He simply smiled the particular smile of a man who knows a secret he is not going to share and told me that our family was special and that Uncle Godric valued us and that I should be grateful. I was grateful. I was also powerful, more powerful at twenty than any wizard in the omniverse outside of Thronegate's Awakened ranks, and the power made me confident, and the confidence made me certain that my purpose was real, that my role as shield was necessary, that Uncle Godric needed me.

Three months later, the Architects of the Final Glyph arrived.

They came from outside the omniverse, which should not have been possible because the omniverse was the container of all existence and there was no outside, but they had found a way, or rather they had been a way, a species that existed in the structural seams of reality, in the negative space between definitions, in the gaps that the omniverse used to separate one thing from another. They were not beings. They were grammatical functions, operators in the language of existence, and their magic was not magic in any sense that any wizard in my lineage had ever encountered. Their magic was authorship. They rewrote the rules. They could change what magic itself was, alter the fundamental syntax of the arcane, make it so that fire spells produced water and healing spells caused decay, make it so that mana flowed backward and enchantments unmade themselves and the very concept of magical defense became a paradox that collapsed upon itself.

They had spent nine hundred years preparing. Nine hundred years of studying Thronegate's magical infrastructure, of mapping the court wizard's defensive protocols, of designing a magical assault that would bypass every known ward and barrier and countermeasure. They had built a weapon called the Glyph of Unmaking, a spell so complex that its incantation required the simultaneous output of seven thousand archmages channeling through a constructed mathematical god, and the spell's function was elegant in its brutality: it would unmake the magical capacity of its target. Not suppress. Not block. Unmake. Remove the target's ability to use magic, permanently, irreversibly, at the level of conceptual foundation, so that the target became something that could not interact with the arcane in any way, a being for whom magic did not exist and never had.

They aimed it at Godric.

I was in the throne room when they arrived. I felt them before I saw them, a wrongness in the arcane field that made my teeth ache and my mana circuits stutter. My father had taught me to recognize hostile magical incursions, and this was not an incursion. This was a rewriting. The ambient magic of the throne room was changing, the rules bending, the syntax of the arcane rearranging itself around a foreign grammar that did not belong. I reached for my wards, the defensive protocols that my family had refined for forty thousand years, and the wards activated, layers of protection unfolding around the throne, around Godric, around me, each layer a masterpiece of inherited craft, each one powered by a thousandfold daily amplification that made my magic terrifying by any standard the omniverse could apply.

The wards lasted approximately one-tenth of a second against the Architects' initial probe. Not their full assault. Their probe. The initial feeler, the first tendril of magical contact, the equivalent of knocking on a door. My wards, the product of forty thousand years of development, the work of a lineage that had served the most powerful being in existence since the dawn of civilization, were brushed aside by a preliminary gesture, and the brush did not feel like effort. It felt like the Architects had exhaled in our direction and the exhalation had coincidentally disassembled everything I had built.

I turned to Godric. He was sitting in his chair, reading. He had not looked up. The arcane field around him was being rewritten, the rules of magic changing in his immediate vicinity, the syntax of existence being edited by beings who had spent nine hundred years learning the language, and Godric was reading. I opened my mouth to warn him, to tell him that the most dangerous magical assault in the history of the omniverse was happening, that the Architects of the Final Glyph were here, that their weapon could unmake magical capacity, that I was his court wizard and his shield and I would protect him. The words did not come out, because something else happened first.

The Architects fired the Glyph of Unmaking. The spell deployed across seven dimensions simultaneously, the seven thousand archmages channeling through their constructed mathematical god, the incantation unfolding in a grammar that rewrote reality as it spoke. The Glyph reached Godric, and I felt my heart stop, because the spell was working, I could feel it working, the unmaking process beginning, the target's magical capacity being targeted for removal, and I lunged forward, reaching for my last defensive protocol, the emergency ward my father had taught me, the one that cost a year of lifespan to activate, the one that was supposed to be the final option, the shield that a court wizard casts when all other shields have failed and the only thing left is to stand between your king and the thing that will kill him.

I did not get to cast it. The Glyph of Unmaking reached Godric and stopped. Not deflected. Not countered. Not absorbed or dispersed or redirected. Stopped. The spell encountered Godric's magical presence and found that it could not proceed, not because Godric had raised a defense, not because a ward had intercepted it, not because any protective measure had been deployed. The spell could not proceed because Godric's magical presence was so vast, so total, so incomprehensibly dense that the concept of unmaking it was structurally equivalent to the concept of emptying an ocean with a teaspoon. The Glyph of Unmaking, a spell that had taken nine hundred years to design, a spell powered by seven thousand archmages and a constructed god, a spell designed to permanently remove the magical capacity of any being in existence, encountered the ambient magical field that surrounded Godric passively, the field that he was not consciously maintaining, the field that existed around him the way gravity exists around a mass, and the spell was nothing. Not resisted. Nothing. The spell's entire energy output, the combined magical force of nine hundred years of preparation and seven thousand archmages and a mathematical deity, registered against Godric's passive magical presence at a magnitude that could not be distinguished from zero by any instrument or sense that existed.

I need to explain what I mean by Godric's magical presence, because I did not understand it then and I am not certain I understand it now, but I can describe what I felt, and what I felt was this. Godric's magical traits, his mana, his magical offense, his magical defense, his spellcraft, his magical comprehension, his conceptual magic, his anti-magic, his arcane perception, all of it, every measurable and immeasurable aspect of his magical existence, was multiplying. Not growing. Multiplying. Every Planck second. Every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. The rate of multiplication was beyond infinite, beyond the mathematical framework that defined infinity, beyond any recursion or iteration or exponential process that language could describe. It had been doing this for forty thousand years. It would do it forever. The magic that surrounded Godric was not a quantity. It was a condition. It was the magical equivalent of the pressure at the bottom of a trench so deep that the concept of depth lost meaning, and the Glyph of Unmaking was a cup of water poured into that trench, and the trench did not notice.

But Godric noticed the Architects. Not the spell. The spell was beneath his notice, was so far beneath his notice that the phrase "beneath his notice" implies a distance that does not capture the actual gap. Godric noticed the Architects, and I know he noticed them because he set down his report and looked up, and his expression was the same expression he wore when he noticed a typo in a document, mild, corrective, the expression of a man who has identified something that needs to be fixed and will fix it without urgency because the thing is small.

What happened next took zero seconds. I know this because my temporal senses, enhanced by forty thousand years of inherited magical development and my own thousandfold amplification, could detect durations as short as a Planck fraction, and the duration between Godric looking up and the event being complete was undetectable. It occupied no time. It happened in the gap between Planck ticks, in the space where time itself has no resolution, and in that gap, Godric's magic moved.

I cannot describe Godric's magic the way I would describe a spell. Spells have structure. Spells have grammar, syntax, components, a beginning and a middle and an end. Godric's magic had none of these. What I perceived, in the fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a moment before the event was complete, was a presence. Not a force. Not an energy. A presence, the magical equivalent of opening a door and finding that the door does not lead to a room but to the interior of a star, and the star's interior is not hot, it is heat, it is the concept of heat from which all individual instances of heat are derived, and you are standing in it, and the heat does not know you are there, and if it did know, it would not care, because your existence is not relevant to the thermodynamic processes of a stellar core.

An infinitesimal portion of Godric's magic, practically zero percent, less than zero percent if zero percent were a meaningful threshold, which it was not, moved toward the Architects of the Final Glyph. I call it "moved" because I have no other verb, but the word implies motion and what happened was not motion. What happened was that the Architects existed, and then the space they occupied was also occupied by Godric's magic, and the two states were incompatible, and reality resolved the incompatibility by removing the less significant party. The Architects did not die. Death implies a process, a transition, a before and an after. There was no process. There was a before, in which the Architects existed, and an after, in which they did not, and the transition between the two states was instantaneous and total and covered not just the Architects' physical forms but their history, their culture, their nine hundred years of preparation, their constructed mathematical god, their seven thousand archmages, their species, their grammatical existence in the seams of reality. All of it was removed, and the removal was not violent because violence implies resistance and there was no resistance, and the removal was not merciful because mercy implies the capacity for cruelty and cruelty implies the target's significance, and the Architects were not significant. They were a typo. Godric had noticed a typo, and the typo was corrected, and the correction was so complete that the document did not remember ever containing the error.

The constructed mathematical god that the Architects had built to channel their spell was the last component to be removed, and its removal was the only part of the process that I could observe in any detail, and what I observed was this: the god, a being of pure mathematical logic, a construct designed to be omnipotent within its operational parameters, encountered Godric's magic and attempted to process the encounter. The god's processing capacity was sufficient to model the magical output of any being in the omniverse. It attempted to model Godric's magical output. The modeling process required the god to conceptualize a quantity, and the quantity was so large that the act of conceptualizing it exceeded the god's structural capacity, and the excess was not marginal. The god's architecture came apart during the attempt, not because Godric's magic attacked it but because the god's own processing tore itself apart trying to hold a number that was not a number, a quantity that was not a quantity, a magnitude that existed outside the mathematical framework that the god was built from. The god died of comprehension, or rather, it died of the attempt at comprehension, because comprehension was never achieved and the attempt itself was fatal, and the god's final state was a string of broken equations that dissolved into the ambient magical field and were absorbed without trace.

The entire event, from the Architects' arrival to their complete nonexistence, occupied less time than a single Planck tick. Godric picked up his report. He did not look at me. He did not comment on what had happened. He returned to his reading, and the throne room was quiet, and the ambient magic was stable, and the wards I had cast, the wards that had been brushed aside by a preliminary probe, were still scattered in fragments across the arcane field, and I stood there with my emergency ward uncast and my purpose in ruins and the understanding settling into my bones like lead.

I was not a shield. I had never been a shield. My family had never been shields. The thousandfold daily amplification that made our magic terrifying by mortal standards, the gift that Godric had given to Blake forty thousand years ago and that had passed through every generation since, the gift that I had believed was a tool for protecting the king, was not a tool for protecting the king. The gift was a kindness. It was Godric giving his best friend's descendants something that made them exceptional, something that gave them purpose and identity and standing, something that let them believe they were needed, because Godric understood, with the wisdom of a being whose intelligence and perception multiplied infinitely every instant, that people need to feel needed, and the kindest thing a god can do for the people he loves is let them believe they are useful.

My magic, the magic that I had spent twenty years developing, the magic that my father had spent his life developing, the magic that my entire lineage had spent forty thousand years developing, was nothing. Not small. Not lesser. Nothing. The combined magical output of every court wizard in my lineage, every spell cast, every ward raised, every enchantment woven, every ritual performed across forty millennia of service, was less than nothing compared to the infinitesimal fraction of Godric's magic that he was not even consciously directing. The magic that had erased the Architects, the magic that had unmade a constructed god through proximity alone, was effectively zero percent of Godric's true magical capacity, and that zero percent was sufficient to erase the entire omniverse infinite times over, and Godric had used it to correct a typo, and the correction had taken zero seconds, and he had returned to his report, and the report was about trade routes, and the trade routes were more interesting to him than the annihilation of the most powerful magical coalition that had ever existed.

I understood then why Godric kept court wizards. He kept us because he loved Blake, and Blake was dead, and Blake's descendants were the last thread of connection to a man who had been his best friend, and the thread was worth preserving, and the way to preserve it was to give Blake's descendants a role, a title, a purpose, a place beside the throne, and the purpose did not need to be real because the love was real, and the love was enough, and the magic was irrelevant, and the magic had always been irrelevant, and my entire identity as a shield was a story that a god had allowed to continue because the story made his best friend's grandson happy, and the happiness was the point, and the happiness was the only point, and I should have been grateful, and I was grateful, and the gratitude was mixed with something that I could not name and did not want to name, something that lived in the space where purpose used to be, something that would live there forever.

Godric looked up from his report. He looked at me with the same eyes, the same warmth, the same attention. He asked me if I had eaten breakfast. I said I had. He nodded. He told me my father had been worried about my sleep schedule, and that I should rest more, and that the trade route analysis could wait until tomorrow. I nodded. He smiled. The smile was real, and the smile was warm, and the smile was the smile of an uncle who cares about his nephew's health, and the being behind the smile could erase the concept of magic from existence with a thought and chose not to because wizards needed magic and his best friend had been a wizard and his best friend's grandson was a wizard and the grandson was standing in front of him with shaking hands and a broken purpose and the god who had broken it did not know it was broken because the god did not know that the purpose had ever been real, because to the god, the purpose had always been a story, a kindness, a gift, and the god did not understand that gifts can wound, and the wound was permanent, and the wound was invisible, and the god would never see it, and I would never show it, and I would serve him, and I would love him, and I would stand beside his throne with my meaningless magic and my inherited title and my thousandfold amplification that made me the most powerful wizard in the omniverse outside of Thronegate's ranks, and none of it mattered, and all of it mattered, and the two truths existed in the same space at the same time and neither one yielded, and I bowed to my uncle, and my uncle returned to his report, and the throne room was quiet, and outside the walls of the palace, the omniverse continued to turn under the rule of a king whose magical capacity was so vast that the word capacity was an insult to it, and the king did not care, and the king would never care, and the king was reading about trade routes, and the trade routes were fine.
